preFix.title: Needle-felted wren
preFix.subtitle: A compact brown wren, approximately 5 cm from beak to tail, needle-felted in warm brown and buff merino
preFix.excerpt: A compact wren figure in warm brown and buff merino, approximately 5 cm body length, with the characteristically upright tail and short rounded wings. Wrens suit needle felting well: their small size and dense plumage require less detail work than larger birds.
preFix.sourceNotes: Needle-felted bird figure technique synthesised from general small-bird needle-felting practice.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: The wren is one of the most rewarding beginner bird projects because the compact oval body shape is forgiving of minor proportion errors and the short, upright tail is a strong identifying feature that reads clearly even at 5 cm. The
barred patterning
on the wings is added last using fine dark brown
roving
and a size 40 needle. No wire
armature
is needed for a perching or resting wren; a standing wren benefits from thin wire legs.

items.name: Merino roving, buff or warm cream (throat and belly)
Merino roving, dark brown (wing bars and eye mask)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Form the body

Take a length of warm brown roving and roll it into a compact ball approximately 4 cm in diameter on the felting mat. Use a size 38 needle to compact the ball, working from all sides, until the surface is firm. Shape the ball into a slightly oval form, wider at the breast and tapering toward the tail end. The body should measure approximately 4 cm from breast to tail end when compact.
Step 2: Shape the head

Needle-felt a separate rounded head approximately 2 cm in diameter. Press one side very slightly to create the suggestion of a short beak area; the beak itself is a tiny rolled cone of dark brown roving stabbed firmly into the face. Attach the head to the body by wrapping roving across the join and compacting with a size 38 needle until the two parts are firmly united.
Step 3: Add the tail

The wren's tail is a flat, slightly fanned rectangle of brown roving approximately 1.5 cm long, held upright at roughly 60 degrees from the body. Needle-felt the tail as a separate flat piece first, then stab it into the rear of the body at the correct angle and compact the join thoroughly. The upright tail is the most distinctive feature of the wren; it should not droop.
Step 4: Add surface colour and markings

Cover the throat and belly with buff or cream roving using a size 38 needle, keeping the boundary between brown back and pale belly soft. Switch to a size 40 needle and add fine dark brown bars across the wing areas on both sides of the body, spacing the lines approximately 2 mm apart. Add a pale buff supercilium (eyebrow stripe) above the eye area.
Step 5: Attach the eyes

Position two black seed beads symmetrically on the head, slightly forward of centre. Thread a strong black thread through each bead and pass the needle through the head from one side to the other, pulling the beads slightly into the felt surface. Tie off securely. A very fine circle of pale grey merino around each bead replicates the pale eye ring of the wren and increases the apparent size of the eye.

items.fix: Needle-felt the tail more firmly before attaching, and compact the attachment point with repeated deep stabs. If the tail still droops, insert a short piece of thin wire into the base of the tail and the attachment point.
items.cause: The tail roving was not compacted enough before attachment, leaving it flexible
items.problem: The tail droops rather than sitting upright

items.fix: Add more warm brown roving across the join and compact with repeated stabs. The neck area should blend smoothly into both the head and the body.
items.cause: Insufficient roving was worked across the join during attachment
items.problem: The head-body join is visible as a crease

items.fix: Use only a few fibres at a time for the bars and space them further apart. The bars should be fine enough to see the warm brown base colour clearly between them.
items.cause: Too much dark roving was used for each bar
items.problem: The barred wing markings are too heavy and mask the base colour

content.text: Finishing

Trim stray fibres with embroidery scissors. The completed wren can be mounted on a short piece of twig as a perch: drill a small hole in the branch and pass the legs (or a short piece of wire through the base of the body) into the hole. The figure can also be placed in a nest made from real moss, dried grass, and a few felted wool strands for display.
